title: Galatians: No Other Gospel
objective: Defend justification by faith against legalism and understand Christian freedom in Christ.

required_passages.text: If anyone is preaching to you a gospel contrary... let him be accursed... a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ... The righteous shall live by faith.

segments.audio_script: Galatians is Paul's urgent defense of grace against distortion.

Listen to Galatians 1-3. Paul rebukes the churches for deserting the true gospel. He recounts his apostolic call and confrontation with Peter to preserve gospel truth. He insists justification is by faith in Christ, not law works. Abraham's example and Christ's redeeming curse-bearing work confirm this.
False teachers pressured Gentiles to adopt Jewish law markers as conditions of full belonging.
Any addition to grace creates another gospel. Faith unites believers to Christ and Abraham's promise, forming one family beyond ethnic barriers.
Key themes include gospel purity, justification by faith, apostolic authority, promise over law, and unity in Christ.

Review question: What does Paul say about the relationship between faith and the law in Galatians 3? Answer: the law is not opposed to the promises of God but was a guardian until Christ came, so that we could be justified by faith.
segments.correct_answer: the law was a guardian until Christ came — now justification is by faith, not the law
acceptable_variants: the law served as a tutor leading to Christ — faith has come and we are no longer under the guardian
galatians 3 — the law imprisoned us until faith in christ arrived
the law's role as guardian ended when faith in christ fulfilled its purpose

segments.audio_script: Christ alone justifies. Next, we explore freedom, Spirit fruit, and practical life in Galatians 4-6.

vocabulary.definition: Paul's central argument in Galatians 2-3 — no one is justified by works of the law but by faith in Jesus Christ; this is the theological heart of the Reformation's sola fide
The Greek paidagogos in Galatians 3:24, often translated tutor or schoolmaster; a slave who escorted children to school and maintained discipline — Paul uses it for the law's role before Christ came
